questions.stem: A 58-year-old factory worker who has smoked 30 pack-years presents with a 2-year history of progressive exertional dyspnoea and a productive cough in the mornings. He has no history of asthma. Spirometry shows FEV1/FVC 0.65, FEV1 65% predicted. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: COPD is diagnosed by post-bronchodilator spirometry showing FEV1/FVC <0.7, supported by smoking history and chronic symptoms. Asthma typically shows reversibility, bronchiectasis has copious sputum and clubbing, heart failure has orthopnoea and crackles, and lung cancer often has haemoptysis or weight loss.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman from a rural area who has never smoked but used a wood-burning stove for cooking for 40 years presents with chronic cough and sputum production for 3 years. She has mild dyspnoea on climbing stairs. Spirometry: FEV1/FVC 0.63, FEV1 72% predicted. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Biomass fuel exposure is a recognised risk factor for COPD. The fixed airflow obstruction on spirometry confirms COPD. Asthma would be reversible, bronchiectasis produces large volumes of sputum, tuberculosis has systemic symptoms, and heart failure would show pulmonary oedema or reduced LV function.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known COPD (FEV1 30% predicted) on long-term oxygen therapy presents with increasing ankle swelling and fatigue. On examination, he has raised JVP, hepatomegaly, and bilateral pitting oedema. He has no history of ischaemic heart disease. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Right heart failure due to cor pulmonale
questions.explanation: In severe COPD, pulmonary hypertension leads to cor pulmonale, presenting with signs of right heart failure. Left ventricular failure would show lung crackles and possible S3, nephrotic syndrome has proteinuria, cirrhosis has ascites and spider naevi, and DVT would cause unilateral swelling.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman who has smoked 10 pack-years presents with progressive dyspnoea and a panacinar emphysematous pattern on CT chest. She has no significant occupational exposure. Her father died of emphysema at age 55. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: COPD due to alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency
questions.explanation: Early-onset panacinar emphysema with family history strongly suggests alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. Asthma is reversible, cystic fibrosis presents in childhood with pancreatic insufficiency, bronchiectasis shows dilated airways on CT, and sarcoidosis has bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known COPD presents with sudden onset of left-sided chest pain and increased breathlessness. Chest X-ray shows a large radiolucent area in the left upper zone with a thin rim. There is no mediastinal shift. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A giant bulla appears as a radiolucent area with a thin rim and no mediastinal shift, unlike tension pneumothorax which causes contralateral shift. Pulmonary embolism, myocardial infarction, and pleural effusion have different radiographic and clinical features.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with a chronic cough and progressive dyspnoea on exertion. He has no known lung disease. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Spirometry showing a post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC ratio <0.7 is the diagnostic gold standard for COPD. Chest X-ray, FBC, ABG, and CT are adjunctive but not diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old smoker undergoes spirometry after reporting mild breathlessness when climbing stairs. Results: post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC 0.62, FEV1 82% predicted. What is the severity classification of this patient's COPD?
questions.explanation: NICE classifies COPD severity by post-bronchodilator FEV1% predicted: mild (≥80%), moderate (50–79%), severe (30–49%), very severe (<30%). An FEV1 of 82% with airflow obstruction indicates mild COPD.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a 50-pack-year history has a post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC of 0.55 and FEV1 55% predicted. He gets short of breath walking on level ground. According to NICE, what is the severity of his COPD?
questions.explanation: With FEV1 55% predicted (50–79%), the severity is moderate. Severe is 30–49%, very severe <30%.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old non-obese woman with a 15-pack-year smoking history presents with progressive dyspnoea. Her father was diagnosed with COPD in his 40s. Spirometry confirms a post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC of 0.60 and FEV1 65% predicted. Which investigation is most appropriate to investigate the aetiology?
questions.explanation: A young patient with COPD, especially with a family history, should be screened for alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency as a cause. This is confirmed by serum alpha-1 antitrypsin level and subsequent genotyping.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old smoker reports progressive dyspnoea. Spirometry shows post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC 0.71 and FEV1 76% predicted. The ratio is above 0.7 but symptoms are suggestive of COPD. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm obstructive lung disease?
options: Full lung function tests including lung volumes
questions.explanation: According to NICE, if post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC is >0.7 but FEV1 is <80% predicted and symptoms are typical, a low DLCO suggests emphysema and confirms the diagnosis of COPD. Transfer factor is the most appropriate next test.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man, previously a smoker (30 pack-years), presents with a chronic cough and exertional breathlessness (MRC grade 2). Spirometry shows FEV1/FVC 0.65, FEV1 78% predicted. He has had no exacerbations. What is the single most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends a short-acting bronchodilator (e.g., salbutamol) as first-line for relief of symptoms in mild COPD with no exacerbations. Long-acting bronchodilators or ICS are reserved for those with persistent symptoms or exacerbations.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with COPD (FEV1 55% predicted, MRC grade 3) has had 2 exacerbations in the past year despite using salbutamol as required. She has no features of asthma. What is the single most appropriate first-line maintenance therapy?
options: Salbutamol as required (short-acting beta-2 agonist) alone
questions.explanation: For patients with stable COPD and exacerbations despite SABA, NICE recommends a long-acting bronchodilator; tiotropium (LAMA) is preferred as it reduces exacerbation frequency. LABA or ICS alone are not first-line, and theophylline is second-line.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with COPD (FEV1 40% predicted, MRC grade 4) has had 3 exacerbations in the last year despite regular tiotropium. He is not on any other maintenance therapy. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Switch tiotropium to an inhaled corticosteroid (e.g., beclometasone)
Refer for lung volume reduction surgery
Add a long-acting beta-2 agonist (e.g., salmeterol)
questions.explanation: NICE recommends adding a LABA to LAMA (dual bronchodilation) in patients who remain symptomatic or have exacerbations on LAMA monotherapy. ICS is considered only if further exacerbations occur despite dual therapy; roflumilast is for chronic bronchitis with persistent exacerbations; LVRS is for emphysema with hyperinflation.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with stable COPD (FEV1 45% predicted, MRC grade 3, no exacerbations in the past year) asks about starting a daily medication. She has no cardiovascular disease. Which treatment is contraindicated in her condition?
questions.explanation: Long-term oral corticosteroids are contraindicated in stable COPD due to lack of benefit and significant side effects (e.g., osteoporosis, diabetes). NICE advises against their use; inhaled therapies are appropriate. Theophylline may be considered but is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with COPD (FEV1 35% predicted, chronic productive cough) has had 4 exacerbations in the past year despite triple therapy with tiotropium, salmeterol, and fluticasone. He is an ex-smoker and has no contraindications. What is the single most appropriate add-on therapy?
options: Refer for lung volume reduction surgery
questions.explanation: NICE recommends roflumilast as add-on therapy for patients with FEV1 <50%, chronic bronchitis, and ≥2 exacerbations in the last year despite triple therapy. Azithromycin is an option but only after optimization and for those not currently smoking; theophylline is second-line; LVRS is for emphysema with hyperinflation; LTOT is for resting hypoxaemia.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents to the GP with a chronic cough and sputum production. He smokes 20 cigarettes per day. Spirometry shows post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC 0.65, FEV1 60% predicted. He has no prior diagnosis of COPD. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Smoking cessation is the single most important intervention and should be offered at every opportunity before any pharmacological treatment, as per NICE guidelines. Initiating LAMA, ICS, or pulmonary rehab is premature without first addressing smoking.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with known COPD, FEV1 50% predicted, is currently on salbutamol as required. She reports increasing breathlessness on exertion. She is an ex-smoker. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends adding LAMA (e.g., tiotropium) if still symptomatic on short-acting bronchodilator alone. ICS is only for those with asthmatic features or frequent exacerbations, not first-line. Pulmonary rehabilitation is beneficial but not the immediate next step.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 30% predicted) has had three exacerbations requiring antibiotics and steroids in the past year despite optimal triple therapy (LAMA/LABA/ICS) and smoking cessation. He uses long-term oxygen therapy. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for lung volume reduction surgery
questions.explanation: NICE recommends long-term macrolide (azithromycin) for patients with frequent exacerbations despite optimal inhaler therapy. Roflumilast is an alternative but is specific to chronic bronchitis and has less robust evidence for exacerbation reduction. Lung volume reduction is not indicated without evidence of hyperinflation.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with known COPD has a chest X-ray showing a large emphysematous bulla in the right upper lobe. She is breathless on moderate exertion. Spirometry shows FEV1 35% predicted. She is on optimal medical therapy. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: After identifying a bulla on CXR, CT thorax is the immediate next step to characterize the bulla, assess surrounding lung parenchyma, and determine suitability for bullectomy. Surgical referral without further imaging is premature.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with COPD (FEV1 45% predicted) has required three courses of oral prednisolone in the past year for exacerbations. He is otherwise stable. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe calcium and vitamin D supplements
questions.explanation: NICE recommends DEXA scan to assess bone density in patients on long-term oral corticosteroids (e.g., ≥3 months). Bisphosphonates are only started after confirmed osteoporosis. Calcium/vitamin D may be given but assessment comes first.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with stable moderate COPD (FEV1 55% predicted) on tiotropium and salbutamol presents with progressive breathlessness over 6 weeks. He has had no acute exacerbations. On examination, there is no wheeze and oxygen saturation is 95%. Which single finding should prompt urgent investigation for cor pulmonale?
questions.explanation: Bilateral ankle oedema in a patient with COPD suggests fluid retention due to right heart failure (cor pulmonale). This is a key red flag for a serious complication requiring echocardiography and further management.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman with mild COPD (FEV1 72% predicted, MRC dyspnoea grade 2) attends for routine review. She has no acute symptoms. Which single finding would meet NICE criteria for referral for long-term oxygen therapy assessment?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends considering long-term oxygen therapy if SpO2 is ≤91% (or PaO2 <7.3 kPa) on two separate occasions in a stable patient. SpO2 91% meets this threshold; the other options do not.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV1 30% predicted) suddenly develops severe dyspnoea and pleuritic right-sided chest pain. His blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg and heart rate 110 bpm. Which single clinical sign most strongly suggests a tension pneumothorax?
options: Hyperresonance to percussion on the right
Tenderness over the right rib cage
Absent breath sounds on the right
questions.explanation: Tracheal deviation away from the pneumothorax is a specific sign of tension pneumothorax, indicating mediastinal shift and impending cardiovascular collapse, requiring immediate decompression. Hyperresonance and absent breath sounds occur in simple pneumothorax as well.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with severe COPD (FEV1 35% predicted) is seen in clinic. She has no oedema or raised JVP. Which single clinical finding is an early indicator of pulmonary hypertension?
options: Loud pulmonary component of S2 (loud P2)
questions.explanation: A loud P2 is an early auscultatory sign of pulmonary hypertension, reflecting increased pressure in the pulmonary artery. Peripheral oedema, raised JVP, hepatomegaly, and RV heave are later signs of established cor pulmonale.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with severe COPD on long-term oxygen therapy (2 L/min via nasal cannulae for 15 hours/day) reports morning headaches and feels increasingly drowsy. On examination, his oxygen saturation is 96% on 2 L/min. Which single clinical sign indicates type 2 respiratory failure due to oxygen-induced hypercapnia?
questions.explanation: Asterixis is a sign of hypercapnia (CO2 retention). In patients on uncontrolled oxygen, hypoxic drive may be suppressed, leading to worsening hypercapnia. Morning headache and drowsiness are symptoms, but asterixis is a specific clinical finding of CO2 retention.
